title: Hungarian Language Basics
subtitle: An introduction to one of Europe's most unique languages

children.children: Hungarian (magyar) is spoken by ~13 million people and is one of the few non-Indo-European languages in Europe.
Hungarian stands out for three key reasons:

rows: Related to Finnish and Estonian, NOT to neighboring Slavic or Germanic languages
Words are built by stacking suffixes (one word can be a whole sentence!)
Suffixes change their vowels to match the root word

children.children: How did a Uralic language end up in Central Europe? The Magyar tribes migrated from the Ural Mountains region around 895 AD, settling in the Carpathian Basin. Despite 1000+ years surrounded by Indo-European languages, Hungarian kept its unique structure.
Hungarian has 44 letters! This includes 14 vowels and unique digraphs (two-letter combinations that represent single sounds).
Key pronunciation rules:
The accent mark (´) indicates a LONG vowel, not stress. This changes meaning! Compare: kor (age) vs kór (disease), hal (fish) vs hál (sleeps over). Always pronounce accented vowels longer.
Hungarian vowels are divided into two groups that don't like to mix:
Rule: Suffixes must match the vowel type of the root word!
Examples with the suffix 'in' (-ban/-ben):
Notice 'angolul' ends in -ul? That's the 'in [language]' suffix, following back vowel harmony with angol (English).
Watch how a single Hungarian word can express what English needs multiple words for:

Megszentségteleníthetetlenségeskedéseitekért means 'for your (plural) repeated pretending to be unable to be desecrated.' It's 44 letters—one for each letter in the alphabet! While artificial, it shows the language's agglutinative power.
Fun fact: Hungarian uses 'kettő' for standalone '2' but 'két' before nouns (két alma = two apples).

In English, word order tells you who does what: 'The dog sees the cat' means something very different from 'The cat sees the dog.' Hungarian works differently — it attaches suffixes (called case endings) to nouns to mark their grammatical role. This means word order becomes flexible and is used for emphasis rather than meaning.
Hungarian has approximately 18 cases, but don't panic! We'll start with the two most essential ones: the accusative and the dative.

terms.definition: A suffix added to a noun to show its role in a sentence (subject, object, recipient, location, etc.)
The case marking the direct object — the thing being acted upon. In Hungarian, the suffix is -t.
The case marking the indirect object — the person or thing something is given to or done for. In Hungarian, the suffix is -nak or -nek.
A word-ending added to change meaning or grammatical function. Hungarian stacks multiple suffixes on a single word (agglutination).

children.children: The Accusative (-t): Marking the Direct Object
The accusative case answers the question 'What?' or 'Whom?' — it marks the thing being seen, eaten, heard, loved, etc. The suffix is -t, but how you attach it depends on the word's ending.

rows: Noun ends in a consonant (back vowel word)
Noun ends in a consonant (front vowel word, non-rounded)
Noun ends in a consonant (front vowel word, rounded ö/ü)

children.children: Notice how the linking vowel (-ot, -et, -öt) follows vowel harmony? The same principle from Section 3 applies here! Back vowel words get -ot, front vowel words get -et or -öt.
The Dative (-nak/-nek): Marking the Indirect Object
The dative case answers the question 'To whom?' or 'For whom?' — it marks the recipient of an action. The suffix is -nak for back vowel words and -nek for front vowel words.

Full sentence example: 'Adok egy könyvet Marinak' (pronounced: AH-dok edj KӨNYH-vet MO-ree-nok) — I give a book to Mari. Here, 'könyvet' is accusative (the thing given) and 'Marinak' is dative (the person receiving).

props.title: Word Order Freedom — Cases in Action

children.children:
Because cases mark grammatical roles, Hungarian word order is flexible. These sentences all mean 'I see the dog,' but with different emphasis:

• Látom a kutyát. (LAH-tom ah KOO-tyaht) — neutral statement
• A kutyát látom. (ah KOO-tyaht LAH-tom) — emphasis on THE DOG (not the cat!)
• A kutyát én látom. (ah KOO-tyaht ayn LAH-tom) — emphasis on I see the dog (not you!)

The -t suffix on 'kutyát' always marks it as the object, no matter where it appears in the sentence. Without the case ending, Hungarian speakers wouldn't know who's seeing whom!

rows: I see the house. (emphasis on house)
I give a book to Mari.
I give a book to Mari. (emphasis on Mari)

children.children: Cases are like color-coded name tags at a party — no matter where someone stands in the room, you know their role by their tag. The subject wears a blue tag (no suffix needed — it's the default). The direct object wears a red tag (-t). The indirect object wears a green tag (-nak/-nek). You can rearrange people in the room however you like, and everyone still knows who's who.

English uses prepositions — small words like 'in,' 'on,' 'to,' 'for,' 'from,' 'with.' Hungarian fuses these meanings directly onto the noun as suffixes. Compare:

• English: in the house → Hungarian: házban (ház + -ban)
• English: to the house → Hungarian: házhoz (ház + -hoz)
• English: from the house → Hungarian: házból (ház + -ból)
• English: on the house → Hungarian: házon (ház + -on)
• English: for the friend → Hungarian: barátnak (barát + -nak)

So Hungarian's 18 cases aren't really scarier than English's many prepositions — they're just packaged differently. Instead of separate words floating before the noun, the meaning is welded onto the end. Once you see cases as 'attached prepositions,' they feel much less alien!

questions.question: What is the accusative (direct object) form of 'ház' (house)?
questions.explanation: 'Ház' ends in a consonant and is a back vowel word (á), so we add -at to form 'házat.' The -t suffix marks it as the direct object.

questions.question: Which suffix marks the dative case for a front vowel word like 'gyerek' (child)?
questions.explanation: 'Gyerek' contains front vowels (e, e), so it takes the front vowel variant of the dative suffix: -nek → gyereknek (to/for a child).

questions.question: Why can 'A kutyát látom' and 'Látom a kutyát' both mean 'I see the dog'?
options: Because Hungarian has no grammar rules
Because the accusative -t on 'kutyát' marks it as the object regardless of position
Because 'látom' changes meaning based on position
Because 'a' is a special word that controls meaning
questions.explanation: The -t suffix on 'kutyát' marks it as the direct object no matter where it appears in the sentence. Word order in Hungarian signals emphasis, not grammatical role.

questions.question: What is the accusative form of 'alma' (apple)?
questions.explanation: 'Alma' already ends in a vowel (-a), so we simply add -t. The final -a lengthens to -á, giving us 'almát' (pronounced AHL-maht).

children.children: Hungarian has TWO complete conjugation systems: indefinite (alanyi ragozás) and definite (tárgyas ragozás). The one you use depends on whether the object of the verb is specific or not. This is one of Hungarian's most distinctive features!

In English, 'I read' stays the same no matter what you're reading. In Hungarian, the verb itself changes form depending on whether you're talking about a specific object or not. It's as if English had two completely different sets of verb endings — one for 'I read (something)' and another for 'I read IT / I read THE book.'
Indefinite Conjugation (alanyi ragozás)
Use the indefinite conjugation when there is no object, or the object is unspecified/indefinite:
Definite Conjugation (tárgyas ragozás)
Use the definite conjugation when the object is specific or definite:
Present Tense Conjugation of 'olvas' (to read) — Both Systems

It's like English 'I read' vs 'I read IT' — but Hungarian marks this distinction right on the verb itself. Imagine every verb has two wardrobes of outfits: one for when you're talking vaguely ('I'm reading...'), and another for when you know exactly what ('I'm reading THE book'). English doesn't change the verb at all, but Hungarian swaps the entire ending.
When to Use Which? — Quick Reference

rows: Látok egy kutyát. (I see a dog.)
Látom a kutyát. (I see the dog.)
Olvasom a könyvedet. (I read your book.)

props.title: The Same Verb, Two Conjugations — Side by Side

children.children:
Watch how 'lát' (to see) changes depending on the object:

• Látok. (LAH-tok) — I see. (no object → indefinite)
• Látok egy madarat. (LAH-tok edj MAH-dah-rot) — I see a bird. ('egy' → indefinite)
• Látom a madarat. (LAH-tom ah MAH-dah-rot) — I see THE bird. ('a' → definite)
• Látom Pétert. (LAH-tom PAY-tert) — I see Péter. (proper name → definite)
• Látom azt. (LAH-tom ozt) — I see that. ('azt' → definite)
• Mit látsz? (meet lahts) — What do you see? ('mit' → indefinite)

Notice how the ending flips between -ok/-om, telling the listener whether you mean 'a bird' or 'THE bird' — information carried entirely by the verb!

props.title: Does Any Other Language Do This?

children.children: Yes, but it's rare! This feature is called 'objective conjugation' or 'definite conjugation' by linguists. Basque (spoken in Spain/France) has a similar system where verbs agree with their objects. Mordvin languages (Erzya and Moksha, Uralic relatives of Hungarian spoken in Russia) also distinguish definite and indefinite conjugations. A few other Uralic languages show traces of it too. But Hungarian's system is among the most fully developed and consistently used in everyday speech — every single transitive verb in the language has both complete conjugation sets.

questions.question: Which conjugation would you use for: 'Olvas___ egy újságot' (I read a newspaper)?
questions.explanation: 'Egy újságot' uses 'egy' (a/an), making the object indefinite. So you use the indefinite conjugation: 'Olvasok egy újságot.'

questions.question: You want to say 'I see Péter.' Which form of 'lát' (to see) do you use for 'I'?
questions.explanation: Péter is a proper name, which always triggers the definite conjugation. So it's 'Látom Pétert' — with the definite first-person ending -om.

questions.question: Why does 'Látom' (with no other words) already imply a specific object?
options: It doesn't — it means 'I see' with no object
The definite ending -om signals that there's a specific 'it' being seen
'-om' is just the default first-person ending
It's a fixed expression with no grammatical meaning
questions.explanation: The definite conjugation ending -om already encodes 'I [verb] IT/THAT.' By saying 'Látom' you're saying 'I see it' — the specific object is implied by the verb form itself.

questions.question: 'Azt olvas___.' (I read that.) — Which ending fits?
questions.explanation: 'Azt' (that) is a definite pronoun — it refers to something specific. So you must use the definite conjugation: 'Azt olvasom.'

props.title: 9. Possession Suffixes — "My, Your, His" Built Into the Word

children.children: Hungarian doesn't use separate possessive pronouns like English 'my book.' Instead, it adds suffixes directly to the noun: 'könyvem' (KӨNYH-vem) = 'my book' (könyv + -em). One word does the job of two!

In English, you say 'my house,' 'your house,' 'his house' — two words every time. Hungarian builds the possessor right into the noun with a suffix. 'Ház' (house) becomes 'házam' (my house), 'házad' (your house), 'háza' (his/her house). The personal pronoun (én, te, ő...) is usually dropped because the suffix already tells you who the owner is!
Here are all the possessive suffixes using the back vowel word 'ház' (house, pronounced HAHZ):
Vowel Harmony in Possessive Suffixes

Just like every other suffix in Hungarian, possessive endings obey vowel harmony. Back vowel words (like 'ház') get back vowel suffixes, and front vowel words get front vowel suffixes. Compare 'ház' (house — back vowel) with 'kert' (garden — front vowel, pronounced KEHRT):

Notice the -j- in 'kertje' (his/her garden) and 'kertjük' (their garden)? Many nouns insert a -j- in the third person forms. Whether the -j- appears depends on the specific noun — it's one of the trickier parts of possession to memorize!

props.title: Building Possessed Forms Step by Step

children.children:
Let's see how this works with 'könyv' (book, pronounced KӨNYV — a front vowel word with rounded ö):

• könyv + -em → könyvem (KӨNYH-vem) — my book
• könyv + -ed → könyved (KӨNYH-ved) — your book
• könyv + -e → könyve (KӨNYH-veh) — his/her book
• könyv + -ünk → könyvünk (KӨNYH-vünk) — our book
• könyv + -etek → könyvetek (KӨNYH-veh-tek) — your (pl.) book
• könyv + -ük → könyvük (KӨNYH-vük) — their book

And you can keep stacking! Add the accusative -et and you get:
• könyvemet (KӨNYH-veh-met) — my book (as object): 'Olvasom a könyvemet.' = 'I'm reading my book.'

Imagine if English contracted 'my book' into a single word — 'bookmy' or 'mybook.' That's essentially what Hungarian does naturally! 'Könyvem' is literally 'book-my' fused into one word. And because Hungarian stacks suffixes, you can keep going: 'könyveimben' = 'in my books' — that's book + my + plural + in, all in one word. It's like saying 'booksmyin' as a single unit!

props.title: What About 'the X of Y'? — Péter háza

children.children:
To express 'Peter's house' in Hungarian, you say 'Péter háza' (PAY-ter HAH-zah). Notice something surprising: the possessive suffix -a goes on 'ház' (the possessed thing), NOT on 'Péter' (the possessor)! The possessed noun always gets the suffix.

More examples:
• a tanár könyve (ah TAH-nahr KӨNYH-veh) — the teacher's book (literally: 'the teacher book-his/her')
• az anya háza (ahz AH-nyah HAH-zah) — the mother's house
• Mari kutyája (MO-ree KOO-tyah-yah) — Mari's dog ('kutya' + -ja)

Optionally, the possessor can take a -nak/-nek suffix too: 'Péternek a háza' — this emphasizes the possessor and is common in longer sentences. But the possessed noun ALWAYS gets its possessive suffix regardless.

This is the opposite of English's apostrophe-s, which goes on the owner ('Peter's'). In Hungarian, the owned thing is marked instead!

questions.question: How do you say 'my house' in Hungarian?
questions.explanation: Hungarian builds possession into the noun with a suffix. 'Ház' + '-am' (my, for back vowel words) = 'házam.' No separate pronoun needed!

questions.question: What is the correct possessive form for 'our garden' using 'kert' (garden)?
questions.explanation: 'Kert' is a front vowel word, so the 'we' suffix uses the front vowel variant: -ünk → 'kertünk' (our garden).

questions.question: In 'Péter háza' (Peter's house), which word carries the possessive suffix?
options: Péter — the possessor gets the suffix
háza — the possessed noun gets the suffix
questions.explanation: In Hungarian, the POSSESSED noun (the thing being owned) gets the possessive suffix. 'Háza' = 'ház' + '-a' (his/her). The possessor 'Péter' stays unmarked (or optionally takes -nek).

questions.question: Why does 'kertje' (his/her garden) have a -j- but 'háza' (his/her house) does not?
options: Because 'kert' is a front vowel word
Whether -j- appears in 3rd person depends on the specific noun — it must be memorized
Because 'kert' ends in a consonant cluster
questions.explanation: The insertion of -j- in third person possessive forms is noun-specific and must be learned for each word. Some nouns take it (kertje, kutyája), others don't (háza, ablaka). This is one of the trickier aspects of Hungarian possession.
